By the March review, we will have retested every unit.
This time next month, we will be running the pilot.
By then, we will have restored full reliability.
We will be monitoring uptime around the clock.
By then, we will have shipped the first certified batch.
This time next month, we will be running the pilot in your region.
During April, we will be monitoring uptime around the clock.
Izabel, thank you for the call. I will be honest: my board wants me to walk away. I need a timeline I can defend, not another apology.
Understood, David. So here are the dates. By the March review, we will have retested every unit, and we will have shipped the first certified batch to your warehouse.
The firmware was the failure. What about reliability -- the downtime is what cost my customers.
The interim firmware is already live. By the end of the quarter, we will have restored full reliability, and we will be holding uptime above ninety-nine percent.
And what happens while my team tests the samples? I do not want silence.
During April, while your team evaluates the samples, we will be monitoring the sensors in real time and sending you a weekly report. Every line is a deliverable with a date.
That is what I needed. We have good rapport, Izabel -- I would rather stay than start over.
Then give us this quarter. If we follow through on every date, we will have won your trust back -- and we will keep your account.
By quarter-end, we will have restored full reliability.
Every line is a deliverable with a date, not a promise with an adjective.

Izabel, it is David Chen, from Meridian Distribution, before our call on Thursday. I will be candid with you. The Halo failure cost my team real money, and my board wants me to walk away and find another supplier. So this call matters. I am not asking you to apologize again; I heard that already. What I need is a timeline I can defend. Tell me exactly what you will have fixed, and by when. If you say the firmware, tell me the date you will have retested every unit. If you say reliability, tell me the uptime figure you will be hitting by the next quarter. I want deliverables with dates, not promises with adjectives. Do that, and I will consider staying. We have good rapport, Izabel, and I would rather you win my business back than start over with someone I do not trust. But I need to see that you will follow through this time. Call me prepared.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Sofia, your operations lead, with the readiness note for the Meridian call. Here is where we will be, so you can commit with confidence. By the March review, we will have retested every single unit and we will have shipped the first certified batch to David's warehouse. On reliability, the interim firmware is already live, and by the end of the quarter we will have restored full performance; we will be holding uptime above ninety-nine percent, with downtime measured in minutes, not hours. During April, while David's team evaluates the samples, we will be monitoring the sensors in real time and sending him a weekly report. Every one of these is a deliverable with a date, not a promise with an adjective. If you walk him through this timeline, we will have earned the right to keep his account. Send me any date you want to firm up, and I will confirm it before Thursday.
